items.title: Gradients as local slope
items.description: The gradient ∇L(θ) at a point on the loss surface is the direction of steepest ascent; its magnitude tells you how steep the terrain is locally.

items.title: Gradient descent on the landscape
items.description: Gradient descent (and variants) iteratively move θ downhill on the loss surface using −∇L; the algorithm is the walker, the surface is the terrain.

items.title: Convex vs non-convex geometry
items.description: Convex loss surfaces guarantee a single global bowl; non-convex surfaces allow multiple valleys and saddles—the core shape distinction for optimization guarantees.

items.title: Loss function defines the height
items.description: The loss function (MSE, cross-entropy, etc.) maps predictions and labels to a scalar height; the loss surface is that same function viewed over all parameter settings.

items.title: Landscape shape and generalization
items.description: Sharp, low training-loss minima often correlate with overfitting; flatter minima can generalize better—connecting surface geometry to the bias–variance picture.
